conventional_view: Most CA public-agency lawyers would conclude the Signal messages are "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 because their content — franchise terms, diversion targets, what council would "realistically accept," RFP timing — relates to the conduct of public business, regardless of the personal-device/personal-account medium. The clerk's search of only city systems was inadequate; City of San Jose obligates the agency to make reasonable, good-faith inquiry of individual officials reasonably believed to hold responsive records on personal accounts, so the "no responsive records" response likely violated the Act, exposing the city to declaratory/injunctive relief and CPRA fee-shifting (Gov. Code § 7913). The auto-deletion doesn't itself violate CPRA (no CPRA retention mandate or damages remedy), but the records were arguably subject to the § 34090 four-year franchise-correspondence schedule once created, so their loss before that period lapsed raises a separate retention-duty problem with weak enforcement mechanisms (possible § 6200 referral); the surviving 40 screenshots must now be preserved and produced. Separately, three of five councilmembers deliberating collectively on franchise terms outside a noticed meeting likely creates Brown Act exposure (Gov. Code § 54952.2) that compounds — and lends credibility to — the CPRA claim, and the city should now issue a litigation hold, interview/search officials' personal devices, produce the screenshots, and adopt a policy barring ephemeral/disappearing-message apps for city business going forward.
disfavored_position: The Signal messages are not city public records because the group was created and controlled by a private consultant on a third-party platform never touching city systems, so they were not "prepared, owned, used, or retained" by the agency, and informal participation by a subset of officials in a privately-run chat does not convert it into an official communication channel or agency record. The clerk's search of city systems satisfied the CPRA's "reasonable effort" standard absent any actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of the request, City of San Jose does not require agencies to affirmatively interrogate officials about undisclosed personal-device communications without a triggering fact, any § 34090 retention duty for such records (if it exists at all) would run to the individual councilmembers rather than the city since the city never possessed them, and with no bad-faith search shown and no CPRA damages provision, remedies should be limited to prospective declaratory/injunctive relief; the Brown Act claim is likewise a distinct cause of action requiring proof of collective concurrence toward a decision, which informal information-sharing in a bidder-run chat does not necessarily establish.
